revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23989927232|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ar:هيميجي جو]][[ary:قصر هيميجي]][[ast:Castiellu Himeji]][[az:Himeci qəsri]][[ban:Kastil Himéji]][[ba:Химэдзи һарайы]][[bg:Химеджи (замък)]][[bn:হিমেজি দুর্গ]][[ca:Castell de Himeji]][[ckb:قەڵای ھیمێجی]][[cs:Himedži (hrad)]][[cy:Castell Himeji]][[da:Himeji-borgen]][[de:Burg Himeji]][[el:Κάστρο του Χιμέτζι]][[en:Himeji Castle]][[eo:Kastelo Himeĵi]][[es:Castillo de Himeji]][[et:Himeji linnus]][[eu:Himeji gaztelua]][[fa:قلعه هیمه‌جی]][[fi:Himejin linna]][[fr:Château de Himeji]][[gl:Castelo de Himeji]][[he:טירת הימג'י]][[hr:Dvorac Himeji]][[hu:Himedzsi várkastély]][[hy:Հիմեձի ամրոց]][[id:Kastel Himeji]][[is:Himeji-kastali]][[it:Castello di Himeji]][[ja:姫路城]][[jv:Pura Himéji]][[ka:ჰიმეძის ციხესიმაგრე]][[kk:Химедзи сарайы]][[ko:히메지성]][[lb:Buerg Himeji]][[lt:Himedžio pilis]][[lv:Himedži pils]][[mai:हिमेजी महल]][[mk:Химеџи (дворец)]][[ml:ഹിമെജി കാസിൽ]][[ms:Istana Himeji]][[mt:Kastell ta' Himeji]][[ne:हिमेजी महल]][[nl:Kasteel Himeji]][[no:Himejiborgen]][[oc:Castèu de Himeji]][[pl:Zamek Himeji]][[pnb:ہائیمیجی قلعہ]][[pt:Castelo de Himeji]][[ru:Замок Химэдзи]][[sco:Himeji Castle]][[sh:Zamak Himeji]][[simple:Himeji Castle]][[sk:Himedži (hrad)]][[sl:Grad Himedži]][[sq:Kështjella Himeji]][[sr:Химеџи (дворац)]][[sv:Himeji slott]][[ta:கிமேஜி கோட்டைமனை]][[th:ปราสาทฮิเมจิ]][[tr:Himeji Kalesi]][[tt:Ак Челән Кирмәне]][[uk:Замок Хімедзі]][[uz:Himeji qalʼasi]][[vi:Thành Himeji]][[war:Kastilyo Himeji]][[wuu:姬路城]][[zh-yue:姬路城]][[zh:姬路城]] {{Short description|Japanese castle in Hyōgo Prefecture}} {{Use mdy dates|date=March 2016}}{{Infobox military installation | name = Himeji Castle
{{nobold|{{lang|ja|姫路城}}}} | location = {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|qid=Q209801|lt=Himeji|1=Himeji}}, Hyōgo, Japan | coordinates = {{coord|34|50|22|N|134|41|38|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | pushpin_map = Japan | image = Himeji castle in may 2015.jpg | image_size = 300px | caption = Himeji Castle in May 2015 after the five-year renovation of the roof and walls [[File:Himeji Castle Aerial photograph 2010.jpg|300px]]
Aerial photograph of the Himeji Castle in 2010 | type = [[Japanese castle#Azuchi–Momoyama period|Azuchi-Momoyama castle]]{{cite web |url=http://sansenya.com/castle_area.html |title=Himeji Castle and its surroundings |publisher=Sansen-ya |access-date=July 6, 2010}} | built = * 1333, 1300 (Himeyama fort/castle) * 1581 (expansion) * 1601–1609 (expansion) * 1617–1618 (expansion){{cite web |url=http://www.e-somen.com/castle/subdata/print/engdata.pdf |title=National Treasure Himeji Castle Guide book |year=2000 |publisher=Himeji Rojyo Lions Club |access-date=July 10, 2010 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110710161456/http://www.e-somen.com/castle/subdata/print/engdata.pdf |archive-date=July 10, 2011 |df=mdy-all }} | builder = * {{ill|Akamatsu Norimura|en|Akamatsu Norimura|qid=Q2828933|lt=Akamatsu Norimura|1=Akamatsu Norimura}} (1333–1346) * {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} (1581) * {{ill|Ikeda Terumasa|en|Ikeda Terumasa|qid=Q1385927|lt=Ikeda Terumasa|1=Ikeda Terumasa}} (1601–1609) * {{ill|Honda Tadamasa|en|Honda Tadamasa|qid=Q3275929|lt=Honda Tadamasa|1=Honda Tadamasa}} (1617–1618) | materials = Wood, stone, plaster, tile | height = {{convert|46.4|m|ft|abbr=on}} | used = 1333–1868, 1945(as military camp) | demolished = * 1346 (demolished for reconstruction) * 1601–1609 (demolished for reconstruction) | condition = Intact, restoration work for preservation recently completed | ownership = | controlledby = | garrison = * ~500 (Ikeda family, soldiers) * ~4,000 (Honda family, soldiers) * ~3,000 (Sakakibara family, soldiers) * ~2,200 (Sakai family, soldiers) | commanders = | occupants = | battles = | events = | embedded = {{Infobox UNESCO World Heritage Site | Official_name =Himeji-jo |child = yes |ID = 661 |Year = 1993 |Criteria = Cultural: i, iv |Area = 107 ha |Buffer_zone = 143 ha }} }} {{Infobox Chinese | pic = Himeji Castle (Chinese characters).svg | piccap = "Himeji Castle" in ''[[kanji]]'' | picupright = 0.55 | kanji = 姫路城 | romaji = Himeji-jō }} {{nihongo|'''Himeji Castle'''|姫路城|Himeji-jō}} ({{IPA-ja|çimeʑiꜜʑoː||TomJ-HimejiCastle.ogg}}) is a hilltop {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|qid=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}} complex situated in {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|qid=Q209801|lt=Himeji|1=Himeji}}, a city in the {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|qid=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The castle is regarded as the finest surviving example of prototypical Japanese castle architecture, comprising a network of 83 rooms with advanced defensive systems from the [[:en:Feudal Japan|feudal period]].{{cite web |url=https://whc.unesco.org/pg.cfm?cid=31&id_site=661 |title=Himeji-jo |publisher={{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|qid=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} World Heritage Centre |access-date=July 4, 2010}} The castle is frequently known as ''Hakuro-jō'' or ''Shirasagi-jō'' ("White {{ill|Egret|en|Egret|qid=Q3060012|lt=Egret|1=Egret}} Castle" or "{{ill|Heron|en|Heron|qid=Q18789|lt=White Heron|1=Heron}} Castle") because of its brilliant white exterior and supposed resemblance to a bird taking flight.{{cite book |first=Nicholas |last=Bornoff |year=2000 |title=The National Geographic Traveler: Japan |publisher=National Geographic Society |location=Washington |isbn=0-7894-5545-5 |pages=256–257}}{{cite book |year=2000 |title=Eyewitness Travel Guides: Japan |publisher=Dorling Kindersley Publishing |location=New York |isbn=0-7894-5545-5 |pages=200–203}} As with almost all Japanese castles from the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|qid=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}} onward, the {{nihongo3|{{ill|Keep|en|Keep|qid=Q91165|lt=main keep|1=Keep}}|Tenshu|{{ill|Tenshu|en|Tenshu|qid=Q49144|lt=Tenshu|1=Tenshu}}}}, the most prominent structure, was used as a storehouse in times of peace and as a {{ill|Fortified tower|en|Fortified tower|qid=Q81917|lt=Fortified tower|1=Fortified tower}} in times of war, and the {{nihongo3|[[:en:Feudal lord|Feudal lord]]|大名|[[daimyo]]}}'s government offices and residences were located in a group of single-story buildings near the ''tenshu'' and the surrounding {{nihongo3|turrets|櫓|{{ill|Yagura (tower)|en|Yagura (tower)|qid=Q603192|lt=yagura|1=Yagura (tower)}}}}.{{cite web|url=https://toyokeizai.net/articles/-/123075?page=2|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190730160807/https://toyokeizai.net/articles/-/123075?page=2|script-title=ja:Tenshu閣は物置だった？｢日本の城｣の教養10選|language=ja|publisher=[[Toyo Keizai]]|date=23 June 2016|archive-date=30 July 2019|access-date=7 April 2024}} Himeji Castle dates to 1333 when {{ill|Akamatsu Norimura|en|Akamatsu Norimura|qid=Q2828933|lt=Akamatsu Norimura|1=Akamatsu Norimura}} built a fort on top of Himeyama hill. The fort was dismantled and rebuilt as Himeyama Castle in 1346 and then remodeled into Himeji Castle two centuries later. Himeji Castle was then significantly remodeled in 1581 by {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, who added a three-story castle {{ill|Tenshu|en|Tenshu|qid=Q49144|lt=keep|1=Tenshu}}. In 1600, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} awarded the castle to {{ill|Ikeda Terumasa|en|Ikeda Terumasa|qid=Q1385927|lt=Ikeda Terumasa|1=Ikeda Terumasa}} for his help in the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|qid=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}}, and Ikeda completely rebuilt the castle from 1601 to 1609, expanding it into a large castle complex.{{cite news |url=http://www.yomiuri.co.jp/dy/columns/0005/lens146.htm |title=A hilltop white heron 400 years old |newspaper=[[The Daily Yomiuri]] |access-date=July 5, 2010}} Several buildings were later added to the castle complex by {{ill|Honda Tadamasa|en|Honda Tadamasa|qid=Q3275929|lt=Honda Tadamasa|1=Honda Tadamasa}} from 1617 to 1618. For almost 700 years, Himeji Castle has remained intact, even throughout the bombing of Himeji in {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, and natural disasters including the 1995 [[:en:Great Hanshin earthquake|Great Hanshin earthquake]].{{cite web |url=http://www.japantravelinfo.com/news/news_item.php?newsid=274 |title=Himeji Castle starts its renovation in April |publisher=Official Tourism Guide for Japan Travel |access-date=July 1, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110324071746/http://www.japantravelinfo.com/news/news_item.php?newsid=274 |archive-date=March 24, 2011 |url-status=dead |df=mdy-all }}{{cite web |url=http://web-japan.org/atlas/architecture/arc16.html |title=Himeji Castle |publisher=Japan Atlas |access-date=July 5, 2010}} Himeji Castle is the largest and most visited castle in Japan, and it was registered in 1993 as one of the first {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|qid=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}s in the country. The area within the middle {{ill|Moat|en|Moat|qid=Q88480|lt=Moat|1=Moat}} of the castle complex is a designated [[Monuments of Japan|Special Historic Site]] and five structures of the castle are also designated {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s110/2212786/_5222/_5237/_5239.html |script-title=ja:国宝一覧 |language=ja |publisher={{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|qid=Q209801|lt=Himeji city|1=Himeji}} |access-date=July 5, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100927071829/http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s110/2212786/_5222/_5237/_5239.html |archive-date=September 27, 2010 |url-status=dead }} Along with {{ill|Matsumoto Castle|en|Matsumoto Castle|qid=Q739612|lt=Matsumoto Castle|1=Matsumoto Castle}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|en|Kumamoto Castle|qid=Q613355|lt=Kumamoto Castle|1=Kumamoto Castle}}, Himeji Castle is considered one of Japan's three premier castles.{{cite web |url=http://www.ktshawaii.com/DestinationArticles/TheThreeFamousCastlesofJapan/tabid/156/Default.aspx |title=The Three Famous Castles of Japan |publisher=Kobayashi Travel Service |access-date=July 4, 2010 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100322220951/http://www.ktshawaii.com/DestinationArticles/TheThreeFamousCastlesofJapan/tabid/156/Default.aspx |archive-date=March 22, 2010 |df=mdy-all }} In order to preserve the castle buildings, it underwent restoration work for several years and reopened to the public on March 27, 2015.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s60/2851146/_21909/siro-weekly-photo |title=姫路市｜今週の姫路城（27th year 2月9日現在） |access-date=2015-01-28 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150215031219/http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s60/2851146/_21909/siro-weekly-photo |archive-date=February 15, 2015 |df=mdy-all }} The works also removed decades of dirt and grime, restoring the formerly grey roof to its original brilliant white color. == History == Himeji Castle's construction dates to 1333, when a fort was constructed on Himeyama hill by {{ill|Akamatsu Norimura|en|Akamatsu Norimura|qid=Q2828933|lt=Akamatsu Norimura|1=Akamatsu Norimura}}, the ruler of the ancient {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|qid=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}}. In 1346, his son Sadanori demolished this fort and built Himeyama Castle in its place.{{Cite book |last=Hinago |first=Motoo |title=Japanese Castles |publisher=Kodansha International Ltd. and Shibundo |year=1986 |isbn=0-87011-766-1 |pages=121–125}} In 1545, the Kuroda clan was stationed here by order of the Kodera clan, and feudal ruler [[Kuroda Shigetaka]] remodeled the castle into Himeji Castle, completing the work in 1561.{{cite web |url=http://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Himeji |title=Japanese Castle Explorer – Himeji Castle |author=O'Grady, Daniel |publisher=Japanese Castle Explorer |access-date=July 11, 2010}} In 1580, {{ill|Kuroda Yoshitaka|en|Kuroda Yoshitaka|qid=Q907019|lt=Kuroda Yoshitaka|1=Kuroda Yoshitaka}} presented the castle to {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}, and in 1581 Hideyoshi significantly remodeled the castle, building a three-story {{ill|Tenshu|en|Tenshu|qid=Q49144|lt=keep|1=Tenshu}} with an area of about {{convert|55|m2|ft2|abbr=on}}. Following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|qid=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}} in 1600, {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|qid=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} granted Himeji Castle to his son-in-law, {{ill|Ikeda Terumasa|en|Ikeda Terumasa|qid=Q1385927|lt=Ikeda Terumasa|1=Ikeda Terumasa}}, as a reward for his help in battle. Ikeda demolished the three-story keep that had been created by Hideyoshi, and completely rebuilt and expanded the castle from 1601 to 1609, adding three {{ill|Moat|en|Moat|qid=Q88480|lt=Moat|1=Moat}}s and transforming it into the castle complex that is seen today. The expenditure of labor involved in this expansion is believed to have totaled 2.5 million {{ill|Man-hour|en|Man-hour|qid=Q1141362|lt=man-days|1=Man-hour}}. Ikeda died in 1613, passing the castle to his son, who also died three years later.{{cite book |first=Ball |last=Jacqueline A. |title=Himeji Castle: Japan's Samurai Past |year=2005 |publisher=Bearport Publishing |location=New York |isbn=1-59716-001-6|pages=32}} In 1617, {{ill|Honda Tadamasa|en|Honda Tadamasa|qid=Q3275929|lt=Honda Tadamasa|1=Honda Tadamasa}} and his family inherited the castle, and Honda added several buildings to the castle complex, including a special tower for his daughter-in-law, {{nihongo|[[:en:Princess Sen|Princess Sen]]|千姫|Senhime}} called ''keshō yagura'' (Dressing Tower). In the [[:en:Meiji Period|Meiji Period]] (1868–1912), many {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|qid=Q92026|lt=Japanese castle|1=Japanese castle}}s were destroyed. Himeji Castle was abandoned in 1871 and some of the castle corridors and gates were destroyed to make room for Japanese army {{ill|Barracks|en|Barracks|qid=Q131263|lt=Barracks|1=Barracks}}. The entirety of the castle complex was slated to be demolished by government policy, but it was spared by the efforts of Nakamura Shigeto, an army colonel. A stone monument honoring Nakamura was placed in the castle complex within the first gate, the {{nihongo|Hishi Gate|菱の門|Hishinomon}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/guide/castle/enjoy.html|title=世界遺産姫路城 城の楽しみ方|access-date=May 18, 2016}} Although Himeji Castle was spared, Japanese castles had become obsolete and their preservation was costly. [[File:Château de Himeji01.jpg|thumb|200px |alt=Photo of the front of the castle complex |Front view of the castle complex]] [[File:Old map of Himeji castle.jpg|thumb|200px |alt=A 1761 depiction of the castle complex |A 1761 depiction of the castle complex]] When the {{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|qid=Q331905|lt=han feudal system was abolished|1=Abolition of the han system}} in 1871, Himeji Castle was put up for auction. The castle was purchased by a Himeji resident for 23 {{ill|Japanese yen|en|Japanese yen|qid=Q8146|lt=Japanese yen|1=Japanese yen}} (about 200,000 yen or {{ill|United States dollar|en|United States dollar|qid=Q4917|lt=US$|1=United States dollar}}2,258 today). The buyer wanted to demolish the castle complex and develop the land, but the cost of destroying the castle was estimated to be too great, and it was again spared. {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|qid=Q209801|lt=Himeji|1=Himeji}} was heavily bombed in 1945, at the end of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, and although most of the surrounding area was burned to the ground, the castle survived intact. One {{ill|Firebombing|en|Firebombing|qid=Q2353049|lt=firebomb|1=Firebombing}} was dropped on the top floor of the castle but {{ill|Dud|en|Dud|qid=Q16821828|lt=failed to explode|1=Dud}}.{{cite web |url=http://www.youmustbetrippin.com/are_we_there_yet/restoring_a_japanese_treasure.php |title=Restoring a Japanese Treasure |author=Lowe, Sam |date=May 11, 2010 |publisher=Best Western's Travel Blog |access-date=July 11, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100524144109/http://www.youmustbetrippin.com/are_we_there_yet/restoring_a_japanese_treasure.php |archive-date=May 24, 2010 |url-status=dead }} In order to preserve the castle complex, substantial repair work was undertaken starting in 1956, with a labor expenditure of 250,000 {{ill|Man-hour|en|Man-hour|qid=Q1141362|lt=man-days|1=Man-hour}} and a cost of 550 million yen. In January 1995, the city of Himeji was substantially damaged by the [[:en:Great Hanshin earthquake|Great Hanshin earthquake]], but Himeji Castle again survived virtually undamaged, demonstrating remarkable earthquake resistance. Even the bottle of [[sake]] placed on the altar at the top floor of the keep remained in place. [[File:Himeji Castle, November 2016 -02.jpg|thumb|200px |alt=Photo of the "Three Country Moat" in the centre of the castle complex |The "Three Country Moat" in the centre of the castle complex]] === Historical recognition === Himeji Castle was registered on 11 December 1993 as one of the first {{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|qid=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} {{ill|World Heritage Site|en|World Heritage Site|qid=Q9259|lt=World Heritage Site|1=World Heritage Site}}s in Japan. Five structures of the castle are also designated {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasures|1=National Treasure (Japan)}}: The {{nihongo|{{ill|Tenshu|en|Tenshu|qid=Q49144|lt=main keep|1=Tenshu}}|大Tenshu|daitenshu}},{{cite web|url=http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s110/2212786/_5222/_5237/_5239/_5242.html|title=姫路城大Tenshu|access-date=May 18, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160624065926/http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s110/2212786/_5222/_5237/_5239/_5242.html|archive-date=June 24, 2016|url-status=dead}}{{cite web|url=http://www.himeji-kanko.jp/en/sightseeing/himeji_castle.html|title=National Treasure, World Heritage, Himeji Castle|access-date=May 18, 2016}} {{nihongo|northwest small keep|乾小Tenshu|inui kotenshu}},{{cite web |url=http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s110/2212786/_5222/_5237/_5239/_5277.html|title=姫路城乾小Tenshu|access-date=May 18, 2016}} {{nihongo|west small keep|西小Tenshu|nishi kotenshu}},{{cite web|url=http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s110/2212786/_5222/_5237/_5239/_5275.html|title=姫路城西小Tenshu|access-date=May 18, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160624090357/http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s110/2212786/_5222/_5237/_5239/_5275.html|archive-date=June 24, 2016|url-status=dead}} {{nihongo|east small keep|東小Tenshu|higashi kotenshu}},{{cite web|url=http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s110/2212786/_5222/_5237/_5239/_5278.html|title=姫路城東小Tenshu|access-date=May 18, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160624072124/http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s110/2212786/_5222/_5237/_5239/_5278.html|archive-date=June 24, 2016|url-status=dead}} and {{nihongo|''I'', ''Ro'', ''Ha'', ''Ni''-corridors and kitchen|イ, ロ, ハ, ニの渡櫓附台所1棟|i, ro, ha, ni no watariyagura tsuketari daidokoro 1 to}}.{{cite web|url=http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s110/2212786/_5222/_5237/_5239/_5282.html|title=姫路城イ・ロ・ハ・ニの渡櫓附台所1棟|access-date=May 18, 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160508173802/http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/s110/2212786/_5222/_5237/_5239/_5282.html|archive-date=May 8, 2016|url-status=dead}} The area within the middle moat of the castle complex is a designated [[Monuments of Japan|Special Historic Site]]. Along with {{ill|Matsumoto Castle|en|Matsumoto Castle|qid=Q739612|lt=Matsumoto Castle|1=Matsumoto Castle}} and {{ill|Kumamoto Castle|en|Kumamoto Castle|qid=Q613355|lt=Kumamoto Castle|1=Kumamoto Castle}}, Himeji Castle is considered one of Japan's three premier castles. It is the most visited castle in Japan, receiving over 2,860,000 visitors in 2015. Starting in April 2010, Himeji Castle underwent restoration work to preserve the castle buildings, and reopened to the public on March 27, 2015. == Design details == Himeji Castle is the largest {{ill|Japanese castle|en|Japanese castle|qid=Q92026|lt=castle in Japan|1=Japanese castle}}. It serves as an excellent example of prototypical Japanese castle architecture, containing many of the defensive and architectural features associated with Japanese castles. The curved walls of Himeji Castle are sometimes said to resemble giant {{nihongo|fans|扇子|sensu}}, but the principal materials used in the structures are stone and wood. Feudal {{nihongo|family crests|家紋|[[Mon (emblem)|kamon]]}} are installed throughout the architecture of the building, signifying the various lords that inhabited the castle throughout its history. The specific style of the castle is a ''hirayama'' (平山城 flat hilltop). Two castles that were built during the same time and shared many of the architectural features are {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Iyo)|en|Matsuyama Castle (Iyo)|qid=Q981357|lt=Matsuyama Castle (Iyo)|1=Matsuyama Castle (Iyo)}} and {{ill|Tsuyama Castle|en|Tsuyama Castle|qid=Q767818|lt=Tsuyama Castle|1=Tsuyama Castle}}. [[File:Himeji-Castle-Painting-Early-Meiji-Period.png|thumb|200px |alt=A depiction of the intricate castle complex |A depiction of the intricate castle complex]] [[File:Himeji Castle No09 055.jpg|thumb |200px |alt=Photo of the family crest of Ikeda Terumasa |The [[mon (emblem)|family crest]] of {{ill|Ikeda Terumasa|en|Ikeda Terumasa|qid=Q1385927|lt=Ikeda Terumasa|1=Ikeda Terumasa}}]] The Himeji Castle complex is located in the centre of {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|qid=Q209801|lt=Himeji|1=Himeji}} on top of a hill called Himeyama, which is {{convert|45.6|m|ft|abbr=on}} above sea level. The castle complex comprises a network of 83 buildings such as storehouses, gates, corridors, and {{nihongo|turrets|櫓|{{ill|Yagura (tower)|en|Yagura (tower)|qid=Q603192|lt=yagura|1=Yagura (tower)}}}}. Of these 83 buildings, 74 are designated as Important Cultural Assets: 11 corridors, 16 turrets, 15 gates, and 32 earthen walls. The highest walls in the castle complex have a height of {{convert|26|m|ft|abbr=on}}. Joining the castle complex is {{nihongo|{{ill|Kōko-en|en|Kōko-en|qid=Q6426700|lt=Kōko-en|1=Kōko-en}}|好古園}}, a {{ill|Japanese garden|en|Japanese garden|qid=Q15835|lt=Japanese garden|1=Japanese garden}} created in 1992 to commemorate {{ill|Himeji|en|Himeji|qid=Q209801|lt=Himeji city|1=Himeji}}'s 100th anniversary.{{cite web|url=http://www.eok.jp/travel/attraction/garden/kokoen |title=Kokoen Garden, Traditional Japanese Garden in Himeji City |publisher=EOK |access-date=July 4, 2010}} From east to west, the Himeji Castle complex has a length of {{convert|950|to|1,600|m|ft|abbr=on}}, and from north to south, it has a length of {{convert|900|to|1,700|m|ft|abbr=on}}. The castle complex has a {{ill|Circumference|en|Circumference|qid=Q843905|lt=Circumference|1=Circumference}} of {{convert|4200|m|mi|abbr=on}}. It covers an area of 233 hectares (2,330,000 m2 or 576 acres), making it roughly 50 times as large as the {{ill|Tokyo Dome|en|Tokyo Dome|qid=Q733748|lt=Tokyo Dome|1=Tokyo Dome}} or 60 times as large as {{ill|Koshien Stadium|en|Koshien Stadium|qid=Q959811|lt=Koshien Stadium|1=Koshien Stadium}}. [[File:Himeji Castle No09 116.jpg|thumb|125px |alt=Photo of gun racks inside the keep |Weapon racks inside the keep]] The {{nihongo|{{ill|Tenshu|en|Tenshu|qid=Q49144|lt=main keep|1=Tenshu}}|大Tenshu|daitenshu}} at the center of the complex is {{convert|46.4|m|ft|abbr=on}} high, standing {{convert|92|m|ft|abbr=on}} above sea level. Together with the main keep, three {{nihongo|smaller subsidiary keeps|小Tenshu|kotenshu}} form a cluster of towers. Externally, the keep appears to have five floors, because the second and third floors from the top appear to be a single floor; however, it actually has six floors and a basement. The basement of the main keep has an area of {{convert|385|m2|ft2|abbr=on}}, and its interior contains special facilities that are not seen in other keeps, including {{ill|Toilet|en|Toilet|qid=Q7857|lt=lavatories|1=Toilet}}, a drain board, and a kitchen corridor. The main keep has two pillars, with one standing in the east and one standing in the west. The east pillar, which has a base diameter of {{convert|97|cm|in|abbr=on}}, was originally a single [[:en:Fir tree|Fir tree]], but it has since been mostly replaced. {{cite book|year=2001|title=姫路市史第十四巻別編姫路城|publisher=City of Himeji|location=Himeji City|pages=494}} The base of the west pillar is {{convert|85|by|95|cm|in|abbr=on}}, and it is made of [[:en:Japanese cypress|Japanese cypress]]. During the {{ill|Shōwa Restoration|en|Shōwa Restoration|qid=Q863412|lt=Shōwa Restoration|1=Shōwa Restoration}} (1956–1964) a Japanese cypress tree with a length of {{convert|26.4|m|ft|abbr=on}} was brought down from the [[Kiso Mountains]] and replaced the old pillar. The tree was broken in this process, so another tree was brought down from [[Mount Kasagata]], and the two trees were joined on the third floor. The first floor of the main keep has an area of {{convert|554|m2|ft2|abbr=on}} and is often called the "thousand-mat room" because it has over 330 [[Tatami]] mats. The walls of the first floor have {{nihongo|weapon racks|武具掛け|bugukake}} for holding [[matchlock]]s and spears, and at one point, the castle contained as many as 280 guns and 90 spears. The second floor has an area of roughly {{convert|550|m2|ft2|abbr=on}}. The third floor has an area of {{convert|440|m2|ft2|abbr=on}} and the fourth floor has an area of {{convert|240|m2|ft2|abbr=on}}. Both the third and fourth floors have platforms situated at the north and south windows called {{nihongo|"stone-throwing platforms"|石打棚|ishiuchidana}}, where defenders could observe or throw objects at attackers. They also have small enclosed rooms called {{nihongo|"warrior hiding places"|武者隠し|mushakakushi}}, where defenders could hide themselves and kill attackers by surprise as they entered the keep. The final floor, the sixth floor, has an area of only {{convert|115|m2|ft2|abbr=on}}. The sixth floor windows now have iron bars in place, but in the feudal period the panoramic view from the windows was unobstructed. === Defences === [[File:Himejijo sama.jpg|thumb |125px |alt=Photo of defensive loopholes in one of the castle walls. Two of the loopholes are rectangle-shaped, one is triangle-shaped, and one is circle-shaped. |Defensive {{ill|Embrasure|en|Embrasure|qid=Q81641|lt=loopholes|1=Embrasure}}]] [[File:Himeji Castle No09 059.jpg|thumb|125px |alt=Photo of two angled chutes or "stone drop windows" on a castle structure |Angled chutes or "stone drop windows"]] Himeji Castle contains advanced defensive systems from the [[:en:Feudal Japan|feudal period]]. {{nihongo|{{ill|Arrowslit|en|Arrowslit|qid=Q16082085|lt=Loopholes|1=Arrowslit}}|狭間|sama}} in the shape of circles, triangles, squares, and rectangles are located throughout Himeji Castle, intended to allow defenders armed with [[:en:Tanegashima (Japanese matchlock)|tanegashima]] or {{ill|Archery|en|Archery|qid=Q108429|lt=archers|1=Archery}} to fire on attackers without exposing themselves. Roughly 1,000 loopholes exist in the castle buildings remaining today. Angled chutes called {{nihongo|"stone drop windows"|石落窓|ishi-otoshi-mado}} were also set at numerous points in the castle walls, enabling stones or boiling oil to be poured on the heads of attackers passing by underneath, and white plaster was used in the castle's construction for its resistance to fire.{{cite book |first=Stephen |last=Turnbull |title=Japanese Castles 1540–1640 |year=2003 |publisher=Osprey Publishing |location=Oxford |isbn=978-1-84176-429-0|pages=64}} The castle complex included three {{ill|Moat|en|Moat|qid=Q88480|lt=Moat|1=Moat}}s, one of which—the outer moat—is now buried. Parts of the central moat and all of the inner moats survive. The moats have an average width of {{convert|20|m|ft|abbr=on}}, a maximum width of {{convert|34.5|m|ft|abbr=on}}, and a depth of about {{convert|2.7|m|ft|abbr=on}}. The {{nihongo|Three Country Moat|三国堀|sangoku-bori}} is a {{convert|2500|m2|ft2|abbr=on}} pond which exists inside the castle; one of the purposes of this moat was to store water for use in fire prevention. The castle complex, particularly the {{nihongo|Waist Quarter|腰曲輪|koshikuruwa}}, contains numerous warehouses that were used to store rice, salt, and water in case of a siege. A building known as the {{nihongo|Salt Turret|塩櫓|shioyagura}} was used specifically to store salt, and it is estimated that it contained as many as 3,000 bags of salt when the castle complex was in use. The castle complex also contained 33 wells within the inner moat, 13 of which remain; the deepest of these has a depth of {{convert|30|m|ft|abbr=on}}. [[File:Himeji castle-hishi-no-mon.jpg|thumb|175px |alt=Photo of the large "Diamond Gate" |"Diamond Gate", the first of the castle's 21 remaining gates]] One of the castle's most important defensive elements is the confusing maze of paths leading to the castle's keep. The gates, [[:en:Bailey (fortification)|baileys]], and outer walls of the complex are organized so as to confuse an approaching force, causing it to travel in a spiral pattern around the complex on its way to the keep. The castle complex originally contained 84 gates, 15 of which were named according to the Japanese syllabary ''{{ill|Iroha|en|Iroha|qid=Q908402|lt=Iroha|1=Iroha}}'' (''I'', ''Ro'', ''Ha'', ''Ni'', ''Ho'', ''He'', ''To'', etc.). At present, 21 gates from the castle complex remain intact, 13 of which are named according to the Japanese syllabary. In many cases, the castle walkways even turn back on themselves, greatly inhibiting navigation. For example, the straight distance from the {{nihongo|Hishi Gate|菱の門|hishinomon}} to the {{nihongo|main keep|大Tenshu|daitenshu}} is only {{convert|130|m|ft|abbr=on}}, but the path itself is a much longer {{convert|325|m|ft|abbr=on}}. The passages are also steep and narrow, further inhibiting entry. This system allowed the intruders to be watched and fired upon from the keep during their lengthy approach, but Himeji Castle was never attacked in this manner so the system remains untested. However, even today with the route clearly marked, many visitors have trouble navigating the castle complex. == Cultural impact == Himeji Castle is frequently known as ''Hakuro-jō'' or ''Shirasagi-jō'' ("White Egret Castle" or "{{ill|Heron|en|Heron|qid=Q18789|lt=White Heron|1=Heron}} Castle") because of its brilliant white exterior and supposed resemblance to a bird taking flight. The castle has been featured extensively in foreign and Japanese films, including the James Bond movie "{{ill|You Only Live Twice (film)|en|You Only Live Twice (film)|qid=Q107761|lt=You Only Live Twice|1=You Only Live Twice (film)}}" (1967), and {{ill|Akira Kurosawa|en|Akira Kurosawa|qid=Q8006|lt=Akira Kurosawa|1=Akira Kurosawa}}'s ''{{ill|Kagemusha|en|Kagemusha|qid=Q854990|lt=Kagemusha|1=Kagemusha}}'' (1980) and ''{{ill|Ran (film)|en|Ran (film)|qid=Q565231|lt=Ran|1=Ran (film)}}'' (1985). In the {{ill|Shōgun (1980 miniseries)|en|Shōgun (1980 miniseries)|qid=Q1778567|lt=television miniseries ''Shōgun''|1=Shōgun (1980 miniseries)}} (1980) it served as a stand-in for feudal-era {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|qid=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle|1=Osaka Castle}}. In the video games {{ill|Civilization Revolution|en|Civilization Revolution|qid=Q1200940|lt=Civilization Revolution|1=Civilization Revolution}} and {{ill|Civilization V|en|Civilization V|qid=Q2385|lt=Civilization V|1=Civilization V}}, Himeji Castle is available to build as a world wonder. It is also to be found as a Great Building in Forge of Empires. In 2023 the LEGO Group launched a model of Himeji Castle as part of its LEGO Architecture Series. === Lore and legend === [[File:Himeji castle-Okikui.jpg|thumb |175px |alt=Photo of Okiku's Well |[[:en:The Dish Mansion at Banchō|Okiku's Well]]]] Himeji Castle is associated with a number of local legends. The well-known ''[[:en:Kaidan (parapsychology)|kaidan]]'' (or Japanese {{ill|Ghost story|en|Ghost story|qid=Q1519335|lt=Ghost story|1=Ghost story}}) of {{nihongo|''[[Banchō Sarayashiki]]''|番町皿屋敷|"[[:en:The Dish Mansion at Banchō|The Dish Mansion at Banchō]]"}} is set in Edo (Tokyo), but a variant called {{nihongo|''Banshū Sarayashiki''|播州皿屋敷|"The Dish mansion in {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|qid=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}}"}} is set in Himeji Castle. There is a disputed claim that the castle is the ''bona fide'' location of the entire legend, and the alleged Okiku's Well remains in the castle to this day. According to the legend, Okiku was falsely accused of losing dishes that were valuable family treasures, and then killed and thrown into the well. Her ghost remained to haunt the well at night, counting dishes in a despondent tone. Himeji Castle is said to be inhabited by the {{ill|Yōkai|en|Yōkai|qid=Q503186|lt=Yōkai|1=Yōkai}} {{ill|Osakabehime|en|Osakabehime|qid=Q17209288|lt=Osakabehime|1=Osakabehime}}, who lives in the castle tower and avoids humans, whom she hates.{{Cite book|last1=Ishikawa|first1=Ichirō|title=Edo bungaku zokushin jiten|last2=石川一郎|publisher=Tōkyōdō Shuppan|year=1989|isbn=4-490-10255-0|edition=Shohan|location=Tōkyō|pages=63|oclc=22591554}} In some legends she takes the form of an old woman (or woman in her 30s) in ceremonial, twelve-layered kimono.{{Cite web |last=Meyer |first=Mathew |date= |title=Osakabe hime |url=http://yokai.com/osakabehime/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210202010259/http://yokai.com/osakabehime/ |archive-date=2021-02-02 |access-date=2021-02-12 |website=Yokai.com |language=en-US}} She can read human minds and control lesser animal-like yōkai, ''kenzokushin''. The legend of the {{nihongo|"Old Widow's Stone"|姥が石|Ubagaishi}} is another folklore story associated with the castle. According to the legend, {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} ran out of stones when building the original three-story keep, and an old woman heard about his trouble. She gave him her hand millstone even though she needed it for her trade. It was said that people who heard the story were inspired and also offered stones to Hideyoshi, speeding up the construction of the castle. To this day, the supposed stone can be seen covered with a wire net in the middle of one of the stone walls in the castle complex. A folklore story is also associated with Sakurai Genbei, who was {{ill|Ikeda Terumasa|en|Ikeda Terumasa|qid=Q1385927|lt=Ikeda Terumasa|1=Ikeda Terumasa}}'s master carpenter in the construction of the keep. According to the legend, Sakurai was dissatisfied with his construction, feeling that the keep leaned a little to the southeast. Eventually, he became distraught and climbed to the top of the keep, where he jumped to his death with a chisel in his mouth. == Visitor statistics == * On 14 April 2009, the total number of visits since the Showa-era restoration surpassed 40 million.[http://www.47news.jp/CN/200904/CN2009041401000201.html 姫路城、入場者４０００万人突破　昭和の大修理から４５年で] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140712141534/http://www.47news.jp/CN/200904/CN2009041401000201.html |date=July 12, 2014 }}（共同通信2009th year 4月14日） {{Columns-list|colwidth=20em| * 1964 – 1,738,000 (Showa-era restoration work completed) * 1989 – 1,197,000 * 1990 – 811,000 * 1991 – 871,000 * 1992 – 885,000 * 1993 – 1,019,000 * 1994 – 983,000 * 1995 – 695,000 * 1996 – 861,000 * 1997 – 716,000 * 1998 – 792,000 * 1999 – 713,000 * 2000 – 662,000 * 2001 – 708,000 * 2002 – 729,000 * 2003 – 814,000 * 2004 – 771,000 * 2005 – 778,000 * 2006 – 899,000 * 2007 – 1,023,000 * 2008 – 1,195,000 * 2009 – 1,561,000 * 2010 – 458,000 (restoration work started) * 2011 – 611,000 * 2012 – 711,000 * 2013 – 881,000{{cite web|url=http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/var/rev0/0072/9570/201561913227.pdf|title=平成26th year 度姫路市入込客数、観光動向・イベントアンケート調査報告書 P3|access-date=May 18, 2016|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160616170524/http://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/var/rev0/0072/9570/201561913227.pdf|archive-date=June 16, 2016|df=mdy-all}} * 2014 – 919,000 * 2015 – 2,860,000 (restoration work completed in March){{cite web|url=http://mainichi.jp/articles/20160405/k00/00e/040/215000c|author=Yoshiko Yukinaga |title=「姫路城来場者、日本一に。過去最多２８６万人」 |publisher=Mainichi Shinbun |access-date=13 May 2016}} }} == Additional information == [[File:Keshoyagura Turret of Himeji Castle.jpg|thumb|''Keshō yagura'' (Dressing Tower) attributed to {{ill|Senhime|en|Senhime|qid=Q1147080|lt=Senhime|1=Senhime}} as part of her private chambers]] === National treasures and sister castles === Other national treasures (castles) besides Himeji Castle include Matsumoto Castle, Inuyama Castle, Matsue Castle, and Hikone Castle. The sister castles of Himeji Castle are {{ill|Château de Chantilly|en|Château de Chantilly|qid=Q766938|lt=Château de Chantilly|1=Château de Chantilly}} in France and {{ill|Conwy Castle|en|Conwy Castle|qid=Q756830|lt=Conwy Castle|1=Conwy Castle}} in Wales.{{Cite web|url=https://www.city.himeji.lg.jp/shisei/0000007173.html|title=姫路市｜姉妹都市（海外・国内）/姉妹城|website=www.city.himeji.lg.jp/|language=ja|access-date=2021-11-14}} Castles that were of the same ''hirayama'' hilltop styles are {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Iyo)|en|Matsuyama Castle (Iyo)|qid=Q981357|lt=Matsuyama Castle (Iyo)|1=Matsuyama Castle (Iyo)}} built in 1603 and {{ill|Tsuyama Castle|en|Tsuyama Castle|qid=Q767818|lt=Tsuyama Castle|1=Tsuyama Castle}}. == Gallery == '''Panoramic overview'''
[[File:Himejijou castle grounds.jpg|thumb|upright 2.5|none |alt=Panoramic photo of the castle grounds, with Himeji city in the background |A panoramic view of the castle grounds, with Himeji city in the background]] '''Views from afar'''
File:Himeji Mt Shosha01n4592.jpg|The castle's keeps and city as seen from [[Engyō-ji]] File:Himeji Castle seen from west 02.jpg|The castle complex as seen from the west File:Himeji castle and bridge.jpg|Keeps and bridge as seen from the entrance File:Himeji castle.JPG|A view of keeps and the lush castle grounds below File:Himeji castle 0012.jpg|Keeps as seen from the grounds below '''Views from below'''
File:HimejiCastleAccess.JPG|One of the steep, narrow walkways controlling access to the castle File:Look up Ido Kuruwa in Himeji Castle .jpg|One of the steep castle walls File:Himeji castle from distance.JPG| Himeji castle as seen from the princess's quarters File:Himeji Castle No09 093.jpg|The keep as seen from within the {{nihongo|inner circle|本丸|honmaru}} File:Flickr - yeowatzup - Himeji Castle, Himeji, Hyōgo, Japan.jpg|{{nihongo|Curved gables|千鳥破風|chidori hafu}} File:Himeji Castle Japan.jpg File:Himeji Castle, November 2016 -03.jpg|Detail of keep File:Himeji castle various views - 2019 8 13.webm|Various external views with tourists, 2019 File:Himeji-Castle-below-2017-Luka-Peternel.jpg|Himeji Castle view from below in May 2017 '''Views at night'''
File:Blue Himeji Castle at night 06.jpg| File:Blue Himeji Castle at night 09.jpg| File:Himeji castle 2009 07 18.jpg| File:Himejijo Kangetsukai Oct09 122.JPG| File:Himejijo Kangetsukai Oct09 198.JPG| '''Views from above'''
File:Himeji Castle - roofs.jpg|Castle walls and rooftops File:Himeji Castle No09 152.jpg|East tower and corridors File:Himeji Castle No09 155.jpg|The castle rooftops and surrounding city File:Himeji Castle No09 128.jpg|Part of the intricate castle complex File:Himeji Castle No09 125.jpg|A mythical tiger-headed fish called ''shachi'' (鯱). This motif was used atop the castle towers as a talisman for fire prevention. '''Views from the interior'''
File:Himeji Castle No09 019.jpg|A {{nihongo|stone drop window|石落窓|ishi-otoshi-mado}} File:Himeji Castle No09 018.jpg|A window for an {{ill|Archery|en|Archery|qid=Q108429|lt=archer|1=Archery}} or defender using a [[matchlock]] File:Himeji Castle No09 044.jpg|An interior room with [[tatami]] mats File:Himeji Castle No09 033.jpg|A hallway File:Himeji Castle No09 041.jpg|Castle windows File:Himeji Castle Interior.jpg|Hallway with displays File:Himeji Castle Hallway.jpg|alt=Himeji Castle wooden hallway|A hallway File:Himeji Castle Weapon Rack.jpg|alt=Himeji Castle weapon racks.|Weapon racks '''Views with cherry blossoms'''
File:Himeji Castle with cherry blossoms from SouthWest.jpg|Taken at Three Country Moats File:090411 Himeji Castle Hyōgo pref Japan01s10.jpg|Sakura at Himeji Castle File:Himeji Castle with cherry blossoms from Himeji Zoo.jpg|Taken at Himeji City Zoo File:Himeji Castle with cherry blossoms from front.jpg|Taken from the south '''Views of the restoration'''
File:Himeji Castle Restoration 0 temporary cover and gantry.jpg|Temporary cover and gantry File:Himeji Castle Restoration 1 temporary cover.jpg|Temporary cover File:Himeji Castle Restoration 3 roof plastering stages.jpg|Different stages of applying plaster to the roof tiles. The plaster protects the roof from water ingress and stops the tiles from being dislodged by typhoon winds. File:Himeji Castle Restoration 4 roof construction.jpg|Roof structure underneath the tiles File:Himeji Castle Restoration 5 keep roof nearing completion.jpg|View of the keep roof nearing completion, taken from the public view gallery '''Views of pictures'''
File:Himeji-jo-112113.jpg|{{ill|Bodhidharma|en|Bodhidharma|qid=Q187172|lt=Bodhidharma|1=Bodhidharma}} File:Himeji-jo-112115.jpg|{{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} File:Himeji-jo-112120.jpg|A fish == See also == * {{ill|Kōko-en|en|Kōko-en|qid=Q6426700|lt=Kōko-en|1=Kōko-en}}, garden joining the castle complex * {{ill|Tsuyama Castle|en|Tsuyama Castle|qid=Q767818|lt=Tsuyama Castle|1=Tsuyama Castle}} and {{ill|Matsuyama Castle (Iyo)|en|Matsuyama Castle (Iyo)|qid=Q981357|lt=Matsuyama Castle (Iyo)|1=Matsuyama Castle (Iyo)}}, also built in the ''hirayama'' (平山城 flat hilltop castle) style * {{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (castles)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (castles)|qid=Q1847513|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (castles)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (castles)}} * {{ill|List of reportedly haunted locations|en|List of reportedly haunted locations|qid=Q3396560|lt=List of reportedly haunted locations|1=List of reportedly haunted locations}} * {{ill|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|en|List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|qid=Q20982902|lt=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments|1=List of Special Places of Scenic Beauty, Special Historic Sites and Special Natural Monuments}} * {{ill|List of World Heritage Sites in Japan|en|List of World Heritage Sites in Japan|qid=Q1156968|lt=List of World Heritage Sites in Japan|1=List of World Heritage Sites in Japan}} == References == {{Reflist|33em}} ;Further reading *[https://doi.org/10.1017/S0080440118000063 Benesch, Oleg. "Castles and the Militarisation of Urban Society in Imperial Japan," ''Transactions of the Royal Historical Society'', Vol. 28 (Dec. 2018), pp. 107-134.] *{{cite book |title=Japan's Castles: Citadels of Modernity in War and Peace | last=Benesch |first=Oleg and Ran Zwigenberg |year=2019 |pages=375 |publisher=Cambridge University Press |location=Cambridge |isbn=9781108481946}} *{{cite book |title=An Encyclopedia of Japanese Castles | last=De Lange |first=William |year=2021 |pages=600 pages|publisher=Toyo Press |location=Groningen |isbn=978-9492722300}} *{{cite book |title=Castles of the Samurai:Power & Beauty| last=Mitchelhill |first=Jennifer |year=2013|publisher=Kodansha|location=USA| isbn=978-1-56836-512-1}} *{{cite book |title=Castles in Japan |last=Schmorleitz |first=Morton S. |year=1974 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm/page/123 123–125] |publisher=Charles E. Tuttle Co. |location=Tokyo |isbn=0-8048-1102-4 |url-access=registration |url=https://archive.org/details/castlesinjapan00schm/page/123 }} *{{cite book |title=Japanese Castles | last=Motoo |first=Hinago |year=1986 |publisher=Kodansha |location=Tokyo |isbn=0-87011-766-1}} == External links == {{Commons|姫路城|Himeji Castle}} * {{Official website|www.city.himeji.lg.jp/guide/castle/}} * [https://www.orientalarchitecture.com/sid/182/japan/himeji/himeji-castle Asian Historical Architecture – Himeji Castle] * [http://www.japanese-castle-explorer.com/castle_profile.html?name=Himeji Japanese Castle Explorer – Himeji Castle] * [https://whc.unesco.org/pg.cfm?cid=31&id_site=661 UNESCO World Heritage Centre – Himeji Castle] * [http://web-japan.org/atlas/architecture/arc16.html Japan Atlas: Himeji Castle] ;Video * [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t2uFc-BeMBg The White Fortress: Himeji-jo] ({{ill|UNESCO|en|UNESCO|qid=Q7809|lt=UNESCO|1=UNESCO}} video on YouTube) *
